questions.question: Explain the difference between ArrayList and Vector. When would you choose one over the other?
keyPoints: Vector is synchronized (thread-safe), ArrayList is not
Vector has legacy status, ArrayList is modern
Vector doubles capacity by 100%, ArrayList by 50%
Use ArrayList for single-threaded code, Vector only when thread-safety required without external synchronization

questions.question: What is the purpose of the Iterator interface over traditional for loops? How does it provide safety?
keyPoints: Iterator provides a uniform way to traverse all collections
Allows removal during iteration (for loop doesn't)
Fail-fast behavior - detects concurrent modification
Works with enhanced for-loop (for-each) internally uses iterator

questions.question: Explain how HashMap handles collisions. What techniques are used?
keyPoints: HashMap uses separate chaining (linked list at each bucket)
When bucket has multiple entries, they form a linked list
Since Java 8, uses balanced tree for buckets with 8+ entries
Rehashing redistributes entries when load factor exceeded

questions.question: What is the difference between shallow copy and deep copy in Collections? Explain with an example.
keyPoints: Shallow copy: new collection but same object references inside
Deep copy: new collection with new object copies inside
ArrayList clone() creates shallow copy
For deep copy, manually create new objects or use serialization

questions.question: Explain the importance of the Comparable and Comparator interfaces. When would you use each?
keyPoints: Comparable defines natural ordering of a class
Comparator defines custom/external ordering
Classes implement Comparable once (natural order)
Multiple Comparators can be created for different sorting needs
Used by TreeSet, TreeMap, Collections.sort()

questions.question: Design a custom collection that stores unique integers with efficient search, insertion, and deletion operations. Explain your choice of underlying data structure and analyze the time complexities for each operation.
keyPoints: Choice: HashSet - O(1) average for all operations
Alternative: TreeSet - O(log n) for all, but maintains sorted order
HashSet: Uses hash table with buckets, hashCode() determines bucket
Collision handling via chaining (linked list/tree)
Load factor 0.75 balances time/space tradeoff
Deletion requires rehashing only during resize

questions.question: Explain the internal working of the Collections.sort() method. How does it achieve O(n log n) complexity? What is TimSort and why is it preferred?
keyPoints: Uses TimSort since Java 7 - hybrid of merge sort and insertion sort
O(n log n) worst/average case complexity
TimSort exploits 'runs' - naturally sorted subsequences in data
Runs smaller than minrun are sorted via binary insertion sort
Adaptive: performs better on partially sorted data (closer to O(n))
Stable sort - preserves equal elements' relative order
Space complexity O(n) for temporary arrays

questions.question: What is the difference between fail-fast and fail-safe iterators? Explain with examples. What causes ConcurrentModificationException?
keyPoints: Fail-fast: Throws ConcurrentModificationException if collection modified during iteration
Fail-safe: Works on copy, doesn't throw exception
Fail-fast uses modCount variable to track modifications
Examples: ArrayList, HashSet are fail-fast; CopyOnWriteArrayList is fail-safe
ConcurrentModificationException can occur in single thread when modifying without iterator's remove()
Also occurs in multi-threaded scenarios without proper synchronization
